narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually stronger than the typical applicant's 3.21—alongside your LSAT score of 36, slightly below the average of 36.7. You also ranked in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, well below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively, and come from a middle-income family bracket. As a white female planning to attend part-time, your background aligns closely with many in our dataset, where whites make up over 80%, females about 44%, and part-time students are rarer at just 7%. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your third-year school decile, which had the strongest pull toward predicting a bar failure, followed closely by your first-year decile. Your LSAT score came next in influence, nudging the prediction further in that direction, with family income playing a moderate role. Your undergraduate GPA, racial background, part-time status, and gender had progressively smaller impacts, all leaning toward the failure prediction in your specific case. Compared to typical students who pass, your low early and later school deciles stand out as bigger drags than your solid undergrad record could offset. While no single factor dooms you, these top influences together tipped the scales. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to push it above average, commit to full-time study if possible to match the majority who succeed that way, and build stronger academic habits early to climb those school deciles next time around. With targeted prep like bar exam courses or tutoring, students in similar spots have turned predictions around. You're already ahead with your GPA—lean into that strength and revisit your application after these improvements. Keep pushing; many have succeeded from here.
